channel.title: NPR Topics: Health
channel.copyright: Copyright 2024 NPR - For Personal Use Only

item.title: Ticks surge in the Northeast
item.description: It's prime tick season, especially in the Northeast and Midwest. The prevalence of ticks is changing the way some people in highly affected areas live their lives.
item.content:encoded: It's prime tick season, especially in the Northeast and Midwest. The prevalence of ticks is changing the way some people in highly affected areas live their lives.
item.dc:creator: Pien Huang

item.title: Most parents track their 18- to 25-year-old kids on their smartphones. Is it healthy?
item.description: A new survey from the University of Michigan asks parents about their use of technology to track their adult children, ages 18-25, including using "always on" location tracking on their smartphones.
item.content:encoded: A new survey from the University of Michigan asks parents about their use of technology to track their adult children, ages 18-25, including using "always on" location tracking on their smartphones.
item.dc:creator: Allison Aubrey

item.title: Inside Ebola country: NPR reports from eastern DR Congo's outbreak zone
item.description: In eastern Democratic Republic of Congo, an Ebola outbreak is reshaping life, with fear spreading faster than information and hospitals turning into places of urgent containment rather than care.
item.content:encoded:
In eastern Democratic Republic of Congo, an Ebola outbreak is reshaping life, with fear spreading faster than information and hospitals turning into places of urgent containment rather than care.

(Image credit: Arsène Mpiana Monkwe for NPR)
item.dc:creator: Emmet Livingstone

item.title: Here's who's missing at the World Cup: Soccer-playing grannies!
item.description: The World Cup isn't the only global soccer event of note. Check out the Grannies International Football Tournament.
item.content:encoded:
The World Cup isn't the only global soccer event of note. Check out the Grannies International Football Tournament.

(Image credit: Samantha Reinders for NPR)
item.dc:creator: Ryan Lenora Brown

item.title: Primary care doctors raise alarm as telehealth companies get involved in obesity drugs
item.description: Telehealth companies offer lifestyle support so people taking obesity drugs can have the most success losing weight. But employers also want the telehealth providers to limit spending on the drugs.
item.content:encoded:
Telehealth companies offer lifestyle support so people taking obesity drugs can have the most success losing weight. But employers also want the telehealth providers to limit spending on the drugs.

(Image credit: Lisa Wiseman for NPR)
item.dc:creator: Sydney Lupkin

item.title: 4 things to know about the new sunscreen ingredient the FDA approved
item.description: The Food and Drug Administration approved a new sunscreen ingredient in the U.S. for the first time in 20 years. It's been used for decades in Europe and Asia.
item.content:encoded:
The Food and Drug Administration approved a new sunscreen ingredient in the U.S. for the first time in 20 years. It's been used for decades in Europe and Asia.

(Image credit: mihailomilovanovic/iStockphoto)
item.dc:creator: Maria Godoy

item.title: Some quarantined cruise ship passengers push to return home
item.description: Some of the 18 cruise ship passengers in quarantine after a deadly hantavirus outbreak have had a tough time in isolation.
item.content:encoded: Some of the 18 cruise ship passengers in quarantine after a deadly hantavirus outbreak have had a tough time in isolation.
item.dc:creator: Rob Stein

item.title: A plan to get lifesaving food to hungry kids was working well -- until it wasn't
item.description: Through an innovative program, parents in Senegal had easy access to a therapeutic food that's a boon for malnourished kids. Now there are shortages. Health specialists say U.S. aid cuts are to blame.
item.content:encoded:
Through an innovative program, parents in Senegal had easy access to a therapeutic food that's a boon for malnourished kids. Now there are shortages. Health specialists say U.S. aid cuts are to blame.

(Image credit: Ricci Shryock for NPR)
item.dc:creator: Jonathan Lambert

item.title: 'Cool Ladies Club' is directed by 10 working-class women. They live up to the title
item.description: They gave smartphones to 10 women from a working-class Indian community to make a documentary about their unseen and unheralded lives. The results are .... pretty cool.
item.content:encoded:
They gave smartphones to 10 women from a working-class Indian community to make a documentary about their unseen and unheralded lives. The results are .... pretty cool.

(Image credit: Mangesh Gudekar/School of Media and Cultural Studies, TISS.
)
item.dc:creator: Neha Bhatt

item.title: COMIC: How excessive heat kills and how to stay safe
item.description: Human bodies have a natural cooling system, but it can do only so much in high temperatures and humidity. Here's the science behind how heat kills. And how to protect yourself.
item.content:encoded: Human bodies have a natural cooling system, but it can do only so much in high temperatures and humidity. Here's the science behind how heat kills. And how to protect yourself.
item.dc:creator: Maria Godoy
